content:
I’ll stabilize the timeline’s scroll gutter so the layout doesn’t jump between the fitted 100% state and zoomed-in states. I’m checking the current timeline board/scrollbar CSS and zoom script first, then I’ll patch the gutter to reserve consistent space.The structure already has both a native scroll container and a custom visible rail. The jump is likely from the native scrollbar only taking layout space when overflow appears, so I’m going to hide the native scrollbar consistently and keep the custom rail’s reserved row visible in every zoom state.The jump is coming from the native scrollbar space, not the custom rail itself. I’m hiding the native horizontal scrollbar in all zoom states and leaving the custom 18px scroll rail reserved permanently under the audio layer, so 100% and 400% use the same vertical layout.
The CSS patch is in. I’m running a focused browser check now: compare the timeline board and scroll rail bounds at 100%, then zoom to 400% and confirm those vertical positions do not change.
The first browser check used an outdated label for the zoom button. I’m checking the current markup and rerunning with the actual control selector.Fixed `editor-loaded.html`.

The timeline no longer jumps between 100% and zoomed-in states. I hid the native browser scrollbar consistently and kept the custom 18px scroll rail permanently reserved below the audio layer.

Verified:
- timeline board height stays the same at 100% and 400%
- scroll rail top/height stays unchanged
- 100% still fits the full 15s timeline
- 400% still creates timeline scrolling
- no horizontal page overflow
